questionText: Prove that the sum of the squares of any five consecutive integers is divisible by $5$.

steps.explanation: Centring the five consecutive integers on the middle value $n$ makes the linear terms cancel in pairs when we expand and add.

steps.description: Write the sum of the squares.
steps.explanation: We translate the statement directly: square each of the five integers and add.
Expanding the leftmost square prepares it for combination.
The next square expands similarly, with a smaller linear term.
This mirrors $(n-1)^2$ with the sign of the middle term flipped.
This mirrors $(n-2)^2$; the symmetric layout means the linear terms will cancel.
Each of the five expansions contributes one $n^2$, giving $5n^2$ in total.
The symmetric $\pm4n$ and $\pm2n$ pairs cancel exactly, which is why centring on $n$ was worthwhile.
Summing the constant terms from each expansion gives $10$.
Collecting the pieces leaves $S=5n^2+10$, with the linear part gone.
Both terms are multiples of $5$, so factoring exposes the divisibility we want.

steps.description: Identify the bracket as an integer.
steps.explanation: For integer $n$, $n^2+2$ is an integer, so $S$ is $5$ times a whole number.
The sum of the squares of any five consecutive integers equals $5(n^2+2)$, hence is always divisible by $5$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Centring on $n$, the sum of squares is $5n^2+10=5(n^2+2)$, a multiple of $5$. Hence the sum of the squares of any five consecutive integers is divisible by $5$. $\blacksquare$
